title: Munster Rugby

infobox.value: ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Rugbaí Mumhan*) is one of the professional provincial [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") teams from the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). They compete in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") and the [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup"). The team represents the [IRFU](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union")'s Munster Branch, which is responsible for rugby union throughout the Irish province of [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster"). The team motto is "To the brave and faithful, nothing is impossible." This is derived from the motto of the [MacCarthy](/wiki/MacCarthy_Mor_dynasty "MacCarthy Mor dynasty") clan – "Forti et Fideli nihil difficile". Their main home ground is [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick"), though some games are played at [Musgrave Park](/wiki/Musgrave_Park,_Cork "Musgrave Park, Cork"), [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Map_of_IRFU_provincial_branches.svg/250px-Map_of_IRFU_provincial_branches.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_IRFU_provincial_branches.svg)

**Munster Rugby** ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Rugbaí Mumhan*) is one of the professional provincial [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") teams from the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). They compete in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") and the [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup"). The team represents the [IRFU](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union")'s Munster Branch, which is responsible for rugby union throughout the Irish province of [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster"). The team motto is "To the brave and faithful, nothing is impossible." This is derived from the motto of the [MacCarthy](/wiki/MacCarthy_Mor_dynasty "MacCarthy Mor dynasty") clan – "Forti et Fideli nihil difficile". Their main home ground is [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick"), though some games are played at [Musgrave Park](/wiki/Musgrave_Park,_Cork "Musgrave Park, Cork"), [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Foundation and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation and early years")]

Munster was officially founded in 1879, at the same time as [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") and [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby"), with [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht_Rugby "Connacht Rugby") being founded ten years later in 1889. The first interprovincial matches between Leinster, Ulster and Munster, however, were held in 1875. The founding of the Munster branch of the [IRFU](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union") was intended to organise and oversee the game within the province and prevent any club bias by providing neutral selectors for the representative side. In amateur days, the four Irish provinces played against each other in the [IRFU Interprovincial Championship](/wiki/IRFU_Interprovincial_Championship "IRFU Interprovincial Championship") and also played touring international sides.

Munster traditionally drew its strength from the clubs of [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick"), with the game popular in the city and widely played at all levels. Teams such as [Shannon](/wiki/Shannon_RFC "Shannon RFC"), [Garryowen](/wiki/Garryowen_Football_Club "Garryowen Football Club") and [Young Munster](/wiki/Young_Munster "Young Munster") built up fierce rivalries with one another, helping push standards in the province higher as a result.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

### Games against touring sides

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Games against touring sides")]

Munster has a great tradition of competitiveness and impassioned displays against touring sides. The first touring side to play Munster were the famous [Original All Blacks](/wiki/Original_All_Blacks "Original All Blacks") led by [Dave Gallaher](/wiki/Dave_Gallaher "Dave Gallaher"), who lined out against Munster in the [Markets Field](/wiki/Markets_Field "Markets Field"), [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick") in November 1905. Munster were defeated that day 33–0. Throughout the years, Munster were to record a number of near-misses and last minute defeats against [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team"). The first tangible result against a touring side was to come in 1958, when the Wallabies were held to a 3–3 draw in [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"). Munster became the first Irish provincial side to defeat a major touring team when they defeated Australia 11–8 in [Musgrave Park, Cork](/wiki/Musgrave_Park,_Cork "Musgrave Park, Cork") on 25 January 1967. Munster were captained that day by [Tom Kiernan](/wiki/Tom_Kiernan "Tom Kiernan").[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6)

#### Against New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Against New Zealand")]

Main article: [History of rugby union matches between Munster and New Zealand](/wiki/History_of_rugby_union_matches_between_Munster_and_New_Zealand "History of rugby union matches between Munster and New Zealand")

Munster first played the All Blacks in 1905, losing 33–0 on the occasion. They have played each other many times since then. Munster drew with [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") 3–3 in 1973 and, in 1978, became, at the time, the only Irish side to have beaten the All Blacks. The 12–0 victory occurred on Tuesday 31 October 1978 at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), in front of a crowd of 12,000, though many times that number still claim to have been present, such was the occasion.[[6]](#cite_note-7) Christy Cantillon scored a try with [Tony Ward](/wiki/Tony_Ward_(rugby_union) "Tony Ward (rugby union)") converting. Ward also added a drop-goal in each half. Until [the national team](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team")'s victory on 5 November 2016, it was the only time an All Blacks team lost to any Irish side in the men's game and forms part of Munster Rugby mythology. A stage play named [*Alone it Stands* (by John Breen)](/wiki/Alone_it_Stands "Alone it Stands") and a book entitled *[Stand Up and Fight](/wiki/Toreador_Song "Toreador Song"): When Munster Beat the All Blacks* by Alan English were both based on the event. Both have been commercially successful; *[Alone it Stands](/wiki/Alone_it_Stands "Alone it Stands")* has had several sell-out runs in Ireland and abroad and *Stand Up and Fight* was a bestseller in 2005.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The All Blacks returned to [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") in November 2008 to commemorate the 30th anniversary of the 1978 match and to celebrate the opening of the new stadium. After 76 minutes of the match, Munster were winning 16–13, but a late try from [Joe Rokocoko](/wiki/Joe_Rokocoko "Joe Rokocoko") meant the All Blacks won 18–16.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

On 11 November 2016, Munster welcomed the [Māori All Blacks](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_All_Blacks "Māori All Blacks") to Thomond Park. The Māori players paid tribute to [Anthony Foley](/wiki/Anthony_Foley "Anthony Foley") by placing a jersey with his initials on the halfway line before performing the Haka. Māori captain [Ash Dixon](/wiki/Ash_Dixon "Ash Dixon") then presented the jersey to Foley's sons.[[9]](#cite_note-10) Munster went on to win the historic game 27–14.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

On 3 February 2024, Munster hosted a world first 'Clash of Champions' against the [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusaders_(rugby_union) "Crusaders (rugby union)"), the 14-time champions of [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby"), at [Páirc Uí Chaoimh](/wiki/P%C3%A1irc_U%C3%AD_Chaoimh "Páirc Uí Chaoimh") in [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"). Munster edged out the Crusaders to win 21–19, having led 14–7 at half-time. The Crusaders were coached by Former Munster Head Coach [Rob Penney](/wiki/Rob_Penney "Rob Penney").[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

#### Against Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Against Australia")]

Like the All Blacks, Munster have played [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team") many times. They first met in 1947, when Australia won 6–5. Munster claimed their first victory over the Wallabies in 1967 when they won 11–8. In 1992, Australia, then-reigning world champions having won the [1991 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/1991_Rugby_World_Cup "1991 Rugby World Cup"), visited Munster as part of a European tour. Munster won 22–19 in a rough encounter in Cork. Ten years later, London newspaper *The Daily Telegraph* recounted part of the legend in a feature on Munster prop [Peter Clohessy](/wiki/Peter_Clohessy "Peter Clohessy"): "The then Wallabies coach, [Bob Dwyer](/wiki/Bob_Dwyer "Bob Dwyer"), who was not a man who readily accepted that opposition sides could legitimately score more points than his team, immediately branded the Munster number 3 a 'disgrace'. It had been a typically rugged, robust and memorable Munster triumph, with leather and fists flying on both sides. Clohessy, who wouldn't generally be known for misconduct, was no more guilty than the next man but world champions are not supposed to lose against a hastily assembled Irish provincial XV. There had to be a reason, an excuse, and Dwyer rounded on Clohessy".[[13]](#cite_note-14)

History repeated itself in 2010 when Munster defeated the Wallabies 15–6, with their Australian fly-half, [Paul Warwick](/wiki/Paul_Warwick_(rugby_union) "Paul Warwick (rugby union)"), kicking all fifteen points (three penalties and 2 drop goals).[[14]](#cite_note-15) The match was played in ferocious weather, with Munster playing into a gale-force wind and driving rain in the first-half. Indeed, the conditions made the half time score of 6–6 all the more significant, as Australia could neither cope with the weather nor the Munster pressure in the second-half.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

#### Against South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Against South Africa")]

Munster have played South Africa much less frequently than they have Australia and New Zealand. The first fixture between the two sides took place on 11 December 1951 in [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), with the visitors emerging with an 11–6 win after a late try broke the deadlock. South Africa returned to face the province for a second time on 22 December 1960, this time at [Musgrave Park](/wiki/Musgrave_Park,_Cork "Musgrave Park, Cork"). They again secured a narrow win against the province, needing two late scores for a 9–3 win. The third meeting between the sides was less of a close-run thing, with South Africa easing to a 25–9 win in Thomond Park on 11 January 1970.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

After the [GAA Central Council](/wiki/Structure_of_the_Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Structure of the Gaelic Athletic Association") unanimously backed the proposal, Munster confirmed they would face a [South Africa XV](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team") in a friendly held at [Cork GAA](/wiki/Cork_GAA "Cork GAA")'s 45,000-capacity [Páirc Uí Chaoimh](/wiki/P%C3%A1irc_U%C3%AD_Chaoimh "Páirc Uí Chaoimh") on 10 November 2022. It was the first time Munster have faced a South African national team in the professional era and the first time a game of rugby was held at the stadium.[[18]](#cite_note-19) The match was a 41,400 sell-out at [Páirc Uí Chaoimh](/wiki/P%C3%A1irc_U%C3%AD_Chaoimh "Páirc Uí Chaoimh"), making it the largest attendance for a rugby match in the province, and saw Munster secure their first ever win against South Africa in a 28–14 victory. Winger [Shane Daly](/wiki/Shane_Daly "Shane Daly") opened the scoring in just the 2nd minute, with fly-half [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") converting to give the home side a 7–0 lead. South Africa hit back in the 12th minute through an [Aphelele Fassi](/wiki/Aphelele_Fassi "Aphelele Fassi") try that was converted by [Johan Goosen](/wiki/Johan_Goosen "Johan Goosen") to level the score, but Munster struck back with tries from [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo") and [Diarmuid Barron](/wiki/Diarmuid_Barron "Diarmuid Barron"), both converted by Healy, to head into half-time with a 21–7. Munster struck early in the second-half, with fullback [Mike Haley](/wiki/Mike_Haley_(rugby_union) "Mike Haley (rugby union)") scoring after just two minutes and Healy's fourth conversion of the night extending their lead to 28–7. South Africa scored their second try in the 62nd minute when [Sikhumbuzo Notshe](/wiki/Sikhumbuzo_Notshe "Sikhumbuzo Notshe") crossed the try-line, converted by [Gianni Lombard](/wiki/Gianni_Lombard "Gianni Lombard"), but the visitors were unable to mount a comeback and Munster hung on to earn a famous win.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### Professional era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Professional era")]

Main article: [Munster in European rugby](/wiki/Munster_in_European_rugby "Munster in European rugby")

On 26 August 1995, the [International Rugby Board](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby") declared rugby union an "open" game, removing all restrictions on payments or benefits to those connected with the game. This was done due to a committee conclusion that having an open game was the only way to end the hypocrisy of [shamateurism](/wiki/Shamateurism "Shamateurism"), and keep control of the sport. The threat to amateur rugby union was mostly prevalent in the Southern hemisphere, particularly in Australia where [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Australia) "Super League (Australia)") was threatening to entice players to [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") with large salaries.[[21]](#cite_note-SuperLeague-22) In Ireland, the four provincial teams were the only teams to go professional, while their smaller constituent clubs remained amateur.

The 1995–96 season saw the [first ever Heineken Cup](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Heineken_Cup "1995–96 Heineken Cup"), a new tournament set up for European clubs. The Irish were allocated three places in the competition, with these places going to Leinster, Munster and Ulster. Munster finally reached the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup") quarter-finals in [1998–99 Heineken Cup](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Heineken_Cup "1998–99 Heineken Cup"), after three years of not being able to get out of the group stages.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Near-misses and European glory (1999–2009)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Near-misses and European glory (1999–2009)")]

Munster's first appearance in the Heineken Cup's [final](/wiki/2000_Heineken_Cup_Final "2000 Heineken Cup Final") was in the [1999–2000 Heineken Cup](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Heineken_Cup "1999–2000 Heineken Cup"), where they lost by one point to [Northampton](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints") at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium").[[23]](#cite_note-24) Nevertheless, the season was most memorable with a 31–25 win over [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") in Bordeaux.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Their good form and bad luck continued in the following season, [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Heineken_Cup "2000–01 Heineken Cup"), with a semi-final defeat to [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français"), again by one point, where a try from John O'Neill was disallowed by the referee, as he deemed the ball to be out over the dead-ball line.[[25]](#cite_note-26) In the [2001–02 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Heineken_Cup "2001–02 Heineken Cup"), Munster lost the last match of their pool at [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique"), but qualified as best runners-up.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Munster beat Stade Français 16–14 in Paris, the only try of the game coming from [Anthony Horgan](/wiki/Anthony_Horgan "Anthony Horgan").[[27]](#cite_note-28) It was then on to [Béziers](/wiki/B%C3%A9ziers "Béziers") to meet [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique") again for the semi-final. Munster were triumphant and went to the final at [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") to meet the reigning champions, [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers").[[28]](#cite_note-29) Munster lost a tight game remembered as 'the hand of [Back](/wiki/Neil_Back "Neil Back")' final, as the Leicester flanker used his hand illegally in a scrum when Munster had a last-chance attack.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Munster also reached the final of the [Celtic League](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") in this season, but lost 24–20 to [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") at [Lansdowne Road](/wiki/Lansdowne_Road "Lansdowne Road"), [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Heineken_Cup "2002–03 Heineken Cup"), Munster reached the quarter-finals after a win against [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby"), later issued on [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") under the title "The Miracle Match". In this game, Munster needed to win by a margin of at least 27 points and score a minimum of four tries to earn a quarter-final berth. They won 33–6 with four tries in a game that has become part of Munster Rugby folklore.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) They again faced Leicester, this time at the Tigers' home of [Welford Road](/wiki/Welford_Road_Stadium "Welford Road Stadium"), and defeated the reigning champions to progress to the semi-finals.[[33]](#cite_note-34) They faced [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") in the semi-finals and lost out on a place in the final after losing by a single point in France.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In this season, Munster won the Celtic League for the first time, by beating [Neath-Swansea Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") 37–17 at the Millennium Stadium, Cardiff.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

In [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Heineken_Cup "2003–04 Heineken Cup") it was more of the same. After an assured performance in the pool stage, Munster defeated [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français") at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") to set up a semi-final date with English champions, [London Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC").[[36]](#cite_note-37) This match has gone down as one of the best Heineken Cup matches of all time. Although leading by 10 points in the second half, and having lost [Ronan O'Gara](/wiki/Ronan_O%27Gara "Ronan O'Gara") to injury early on, they succumbed to two Wasps tries in injury time, resulting in a Wasps v Toulouse final.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) Munster finished in a disappointing seventh position in the 2003–04 season of the Celtic League.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

In [2004–05](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Heineken_Cup "2004–05 Heineken Cup"), after a shaky performance in the pool stage, Munster qualified as 5th seeds and played [Biarritz](/wiki/Biarritz_Olympique "Biarritz Olympique") away. The match was played at [Real Sociedad](/wiki/Real_Sociedad "Real Sociedad")'s ground, the [Anoeta Stadium](/wiki/Estadio_Anoeta "Estadio Anoeta"), in [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián"), Spain – the first Heineken Cup game ever played in Spain. Biarritz won 19–10 to avenge a 38–29 defeat at the same stage in 2001.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Munster finished second in the 2004–05 Celtic League season but won the [Celtic Cup](/wiki/Celtic_Cup_(rugby_union) "Celtic Cup (rugby union)"), defeating [Llanelli Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") 27–16 in the final.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/HeinekenCupFinal06MunsterVBiarritz200506SmallByMike.JPG/250px-HeinekenCupFinal06MunsterVBiarritz200506SmallByMike.JPG)](/wiki/File:HeinekenCupFinal06MunsterVBiarritz200506SmallByMike.JPG)

Inside the Millennium Stadium for the 2006 final, where over 65,000 Munster fans were present

In [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Heineken_Cup "2005–06 Heineken Cup"), Munster qualified to the final of the Heineken Cup, having overcome rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") 30–6 in the semi-final at [Lansdowne Road](/wiki/Lansdowne_Road "Lansdowne Road").[[42]](#cite_note-43) The [final](/wiki/2006_Heineken_Cup_Final "2006 Heineken Cup Final") was held at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") against Biarritz. Munster won 23–19 to become European Champions for the first time.[[43]](#cite_note-44) Munster finished one place lower than the previous season in the Celtic League, finishing third overall.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

The [2006–07 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Heineken_Cup "2006–07 Heineken Cup") was a disappointing tournament for Munster, as they lost their previously unbeaten European record at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), losing to [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") in the group stages in January 2007,[[45]](#cite_note-46) and later lost their quarter-final to [Llanelli Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets").[[46]](#cite_note-47) Munster's performance in the Celtic League was equally disappointing, finishing sixth overall.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

In 2007, Munster signed [Doug Howlett](/wiki/Doug_Howlett "Doug Howlett"), the all-time leading try scorer for [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team").[[48]](#cite_note-49) Howlett joined the squad after the [2007 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup").[[49]](#cite_note-50) That season's [Heineken Cup](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Heineken_Cup "2007–08 Heineken Cup") saw Munster finish top in their group,[[50]](#cite_note-51) and they went on to reach the final for the second time in three years, beating [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") in the semi-final.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The [final](/wiki/2008_Heineken_Cup_Final "2008 Heineken Cup Final"), again held at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium"), saw Munster defeat Toulouse 16–13 to claim their second Heineken Cup title in 3 years.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) Munster finished third in the Celtic League in the 2007–08 season. In May 2008, Director of Coaching [Declan Kidney](/wiki/Declan_Kidney "Declan Kidney") left to take up the head coach job with [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team").[[54]](#cite_note-55) Munster ensured continuity by promoting Australian [Tony McGahan](/wiki/Tony_McGahan "Tony McGahan") to the position from within the coaching setup.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In the [2008–09 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Heineken_Cup "2008–09 Heineken Cup"), Munster once again topped their group and hammered [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") 43–9 to advance to a semi-final against arch-rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby").[[56]](#cite_note-57) Many had fancied Munster to become only the second team to win back-to-back Heineken Cups, but a dominant Leinster side gained revenge for their 2006 semi-final defeat against Munster with a 25–6 win in [Croke Park](/wiki/Croke_Park "Croke Park"), in front of a then-world record 82,208 crowd.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59) Munster clinched the [Celtic League](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Celtic_League "2008–09 Celtic League") for the second time in their history, after their closest challengers [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") beat [Dragons](/wiki/Dragons_RFC "Dragons RFC") but failed to claim a bonus point. This handed the title to Munster who could not be overtaken at the top of the table.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### The McGahan and Penney years (2009–2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The McGahan and Penney years (2009–2014)")]

The [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Heineken_Cup "2009–10 Heineken Cup") season saw Munster finish top of their Heineken Cup pool once again. Victories over [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints"), French [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14") champions [Perpignan](/wiki/USA_Perpignan "USA Perpignan") and Italian side [Benetton](/wiki/Benetton_Rugby "Benetton Rugby") saw Munster qualify for the Heineken Cup quarter-finals for a record 12th consecutive year.[[60]](#cite_note-61) The match took place at Thomond Park, where Munster played Northampton Saints for the third time that season and won 33–19.[[61]](#cite_note-62) They lost 18–7 in the semi-finals against Biarritz at the Anoeta.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Munster came 4th in the Celtic League, but because this season saw the introduction of a play-off system for the top four teams, Munster met and lost 16–6 to Leinster in the play-off semi-final.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

The [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Munster_Rugby_season "2010–11 Munster Rugby season") season saw Munster drawn in [pool 3](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Heineken_Cup "2010–11 Heineken Cup") of the Heineken Cup alongside [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)"), [London Irish](/wiki/London_Irish "London Irish") and [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais"). Munster lost 23–17 away to London Irish,[[64]](#cite_note-65) before defeating Toulon 45–18 at Thomond Park.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Munster defeated Ospreys 22–16,[[66]](#cite_note-67) but lost the reverse fixture at Liberty Stadium 19–15.[[67]](#cite_note-68) In round 5 Munster went to Toulon, losing 32–16. As a result, Munster failed to qualify for the quarter-finals of the Heineken Cup for the first time in 13 years.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Munster won their final pool game, at home to London Irish, 28–14, and qualified for the [2010–11 European Challenge Cup](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_European_Challenge_Cup "2010–11 European Challenge Cup") quarter-finals.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Munster defeated Leinster 24–23 on 2 April 2011 in the Celtic League, ending a run of 5 straight defeats against their provincial rivals.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Munster beat [Brive](/wiki/CA_Brive "CA Brive") 42–37 in their Challenge Cup quarter-final to qualify for the semi-final against [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") on 30 April.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Munster lost the semi-final in [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") 20–12.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Munster finished first in the [2010–11 Celtic League](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Celtic_League "2010–11 Celtic League"). They beat Ospreys 18–11 in their semi-final to set up a [Grand Final](/wiki/2011_Magners_League_Grand_Final "2011 Magners League Grand Final") with [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby"),[[73]](#cite_note-74) which Munster won 19–9, securing a third [Celtic League](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") title.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

Munster were drawn in pool one for the [2011–12 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Heineken_Cup "2011–12 Heineken Cup"), alongside [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints"), [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") and [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique"). They beat Northampton 23–21 in the first pool game at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), after an 83rd minute drop-goal from [Ronan O'Gara](/wiki/Ronan_O%27Gara "Ronan O'Gara").[[76]](#cite_note-77) In their second pool game, Munster beat Castres 24–27, with O'Gara again scoring an overtime drop-goal to secure victory.[[77]](#cite_note-78) Munster won their third pool game, away to [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets"), 17–14.[[78]](#cite_note-79) In the return fixture a week later, Munster won 19–13.[[79]](#cite_note-80) A 26–10 win over Castres on 14 January 2012 ensured that Munster qualified for the quarter-finals of the 2011–12 Heineken Cup.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Munster ended their 2011–12 Heineken Cup pool fixtures with a 51–36 victory against Northampton Saints, securing the top seed in the quarter-finals and winning 6 out of 6 pool matches for the first time.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Munster lost their quarter-final against Ulster 22–16, losing just their second match at home in the Heineken Cup.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Munster finished third in the [2011–12 Pro12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Pro12 "2011–12 Pro12"), and played [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") away in the play-off semi-finals, losing 45–10.[[83]](#cite_note-84) It was announced on 22 February 2012 that McGahan would be leaving Munster at the end of the 2011–12 season,[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86) to take up a role of coaching co-ordinator on [Australia's](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team") management team. [Rob Penney](/wiki/Rob_Penney "Rob Penney"), coach of [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_representative_rugby_union_team "Canterbury representative rugby union team") and [New Zealand U20](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_under-20_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national under-20 rugby union team"), was chosen to succeed McGahan, being unveiled as the next Munster coach on 2 May 2012.[[86]](#cite_note-Penney_Appointed-87)

Munster were again drawn in pool one for the [2012–13 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Heineken_Cup "2012–13 Heineken Cup"), with [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C."), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") and [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92").[[87]](#cite_note-88) The campaign opened with a 22–17 away defeat at the hands of [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92").[[88]](#cite_note-89) Munster won their second game, at home to [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby"), 33–0.[[89]](#cite_note-90) The December back-to-back games began with a home fixture against [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C."), which Munster won 15–9.[[90]](#cite_note-91) In the reverse fixture, Munster lost 19–13.[[91]](#cite_note-92) In the fifth round of pool fixtures, Munster beat [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") 26–17.[[92]](#cite_note-93) Munster had to win their final pool game, against [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92"), with a try bonus-point to have a chance of qualifying for the quarter-finals. Munster won the game 29–6, scoring five tries, including a hat-trick from [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo").[[93]](#cite_note-94) [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers")' 9–5 victory over [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") ensured that Munster qualified as a second-best runner-up, and they played [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") in the quarter-finals. Munster won the quarter-final 18–12,[[94]](#cite_note-95) advancing to the semi-finals, which they lost 16–10 to [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") on 27 April 2013.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Munster finished sixth in the [2012–13 Pro12](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Pro12 "2012–13 Pro12").

Munster were drawn in pool 6 for the [2013–14 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Heineken_Cup "2013–14 Heineken Cup"), alongside [Perpignan](/wiki/USA_Perpignan "USA Perpignan"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") and [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby").[[96]](#cite_note-97) Edinburgh beat Munster 29–23 in the opening pool fixture on 12 October 2013.[[97]](#cite_note-98) In their second pool game on 19 October 2013, Munster beat Gloucester 26–10.[[98]](#cite_note-99) Munster beat Perpignan 36–8 in round 3,[[99]](#cite_note-100) and, in the round 4 reverse fixture on 14 December 2013, won 18–17 in Perpignan.[[100]](#cite_note-101) Munster beat [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby") 20–7 at [Kingsholm](/wiki/Kingsholm_Stadium "Kingsholm Stadium") on 11 January 2014, a win that secured quarter-final qualification.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Munster beat [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") 38–6 on 19 January 2014 in round 6, a bonus-point win that secured a home quarter-final.[[102]](#cite_note-103) On 6 February 2014, it was announced that Penney and backs coach [Simon Mannix](/wiki/Simon_Mannix "Simon Mannix") would be leaving Munster at the end of the 2013–14 season.[[103]](#cite_note-104) Former captain and then-forwards coach [Anthony Foley](/wiki/Anthony_Foley "Anthony Foley") was confirmed as the next Munster coach on 19 February 2014.[[104]](#cite_note-105) In their quarter-final, Munster beat [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") 47–23.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Munster lost 24–16 to [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") in the semi-final on 27 April 2014.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Munster finished third in the [2013–14 Pro12](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Pro12 "2013–14 Pro12"),[[107]](#cite_note-108) but lost 16–15 to [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors") in the semi–final.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

### Champions Cup begins (2014–2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Champions Cup begins (2014–2017)")]

Munster were drawn in pool 1 of the [2014–15 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2014–15 European Rugby Champions Cup"), alongside [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C."), [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") and [Sale Sharks](/wiki/Sale_Sharks "Sale Sharks").[[109]](#cite_note-110) A late drop-goal from [Ian Keatley](/wiki/Ian_Keatley "Ian Keatley") gave Munster a 27–26 away win against [Sale Sharks](/wiki/Sale_Sharks "Sale Sharks") in round 1 on 18 October 2014.[[110]](#cite_note-111) Munster beats [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") 14–3 in round 2 on 24 October 2014.[[111]](#cite_note-112) [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne") beat Munster 16–9 at Thomond Park in round 3 on 6 December 2014, becoming the first French team to beat Munster at their home stadium.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In the reverse fixture on 14 December 2014, Clermont beat Munster 26–19.[[113]](#cite_note-114) Saracens beat Munster 33–10 on 17 January 2015 in round 5, a defeat which meant Munster failed to qualify for the knockout stages for only the second time in 17 seasons.[[114]](#cite_note-115) In round 6, Munster beat Sale Sharks 65–10, a win that was their 100th in Europe.[[115]](#cite_note-116) Munster finished second on the [2014–15 Pro12](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Pro12 "2014–15 Pro12").[[116]](#cite_note-117) In the play-off semi-final, Munster beat [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") 21–18.[[117]](#cite_note-118) Munster were beaten 31–13 by [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors") in the [2015 Pro12 Grand Final](/wiki/2015_Pro12_Grand_Final "2015 Pro12 Grand Final") on 30 May 2015.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

Munster were drawn in pool 4 of the [2015–16 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2015–16 European Rugby Champions Cup"), alongside [Stade Français](/wiki/Stade_Fran%C3%A7ais "Stade Français"), [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") and [Benetton](/wiki/Benetton_Rugby "Benetton Rugby").[[119]](#cite_note-120) On 14 November 2015, Munster beat Treviso 32–7 in their opening pool game.[[120]](#cite_note-121) Munster's second pool game, against Stade Français, was postponed following the [November 2015 Paris attacks](/wiki/November_2015_Paris_attacks "November 2015 Paris attacks"). The match was scheduled to be played at the [Stade Jean-Bouin](/wiki/Stade_Jean-Bouin_(Paris) "Stade Jean-Bouin (Paris)") in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[121]](#cite_note-122) Munster lost 31–19 to Leicester Tigers in round 3 on 12 December 2015.[[122]](#cite_note-123) In the return fixture on 20 December 2015, Leicester beat Munster 17–6.[[123]](#cite_note-124) On 9 January 2016, Munster lost 27–7 to Stade Français in the re-arranged second pool game. A week later, Munster beat Stade Français 26–13.[[124]](#cite_note-125) In their final pool game on 24 January 2016, Munster beat Treviso 28–5.[[125]](#cite_note-126) In April 2016, it was confirmed that former Springbok [Rassie Erasmus](/wiki/Rassie_Erasmus "Rassie Erasmus") would be joining Munster as the director of rugby on a three-year contract, beginning on 1 July 2016.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Munster finished 6th in the [2015–16 Pro12](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Pro12 "2015–16 Pro12").[[127]](#cite_note-128)

Munster were drawn in pool 1 of the [2016–17 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2016–17 European Rugby Champions Cup"), alongside [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92"), [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") and [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors").[[128]](#cite_note-129) In October 2016, Munster's head coach and former captain [Anthony Foley](/wiki/Anthony_Foley "Anthony Foley") died while the team were in Paris for the first pool game against Racing 92. Due to the sad and sudden passing of Foley, the fixture was rescheduled.[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-131) On 22 October 2016, in the first game since Foley's death, Munster beat Glasgow 38–17 at a sold-out Thomond Park. Tributes were paid to Foley before, during and after the game and the number 8 jersey was retired for the game, with [CJ Stander](/wiki/CJ_Stander "CJ Stander") wearing the number 24 for the occasion.[[131]](#cite_note-132) On 10 December 2016, Munster beat Leicester 38–0 in round 3 of the Champions Cup.[[132]](#cite_note-133) On 17 December 2016, Leicester won the reverse fixture in [Welford Road](/wiki/Welford_Road_Stadium "Welford Road Stadium") 18–16 after a last-minute penalty from [Owen Williams](/wiki/Owen_Williams_(rugby_union,_born_1992) "Owen Williams (rugby union, born 1992)").[[133]](#cite_note-134) On 7 January 2017, in the rescheduled round 1 fixture, Munster beat Racing 92 32–7 away from home. [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo")'s opening try was Munster's 400th in European competition.[[134]](#cite_note-135) On 14 January 2017, Munster beat Glasgow 14–12 away from home to secure qualification for the quarter-finals.[[135]](#cite_note-136) On 21 January 2017, in front of 26,200 spectators in Thomond Park, Munster beat Racing 92 22–10 in round 6 of the pool stage, securing a home quarter-final[[136]](#cite_note-137) On 1 April 2017, in the quarter-final, Munster beat [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") 41–16 to progress to the semi-finals.[[137]](#cite_note-138) On 22 April 2017, Munster were beaten 26–10 by defending champions [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") in the Champions Cup semi-final, which was held in the [Aviva Stadium](/wiki/Aviva_Stadium "Aviva Stadium"), [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin").[[138]](#cite_note-139) Munster finished 1st after the 22-round regular season of the [2016–17 Pro12](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Pro12 "2016–17 Pro12"), with a record total of 86 points.[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141) On 20 May 2017, in their play-off semi-final, Munster beat [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") 23–3 in [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park").[[141]](#cite_note-142) 7 days later, in the [2017 Pro12 Grand Final](/wiki/2017_Pro12_Grand_Final "2017 Pro12 Grand Final"), Munster lost 46–22 to [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets").[[142]](#cite_note-143)

### Pro14 expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pro14 expansion")]

When the draw for the [2017–18 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2017–18 European Rugby Champions Cup") was made, Munster were drawn in pool 4 alongside [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers"), [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92") and [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique"). It marked the third season in a row in which Munster faced Leicester and the second season in a row in which they will play Racing 92.[[143]](#cite_note-144) On 30 June 2017, it was confirmed after weeks of speculation that director of rugby [Rassie Erasmus](/wiki/Rassie_Erasmus "Rassie Erasmus") and defence coach [Jacques Nienaber](/wiki/Jacques_Nienaber "Jacques Nienaber") would leave the province in December of that year.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

In August 2017, Celtic Rugby Limited and the [South African Rugby Union](/wiki/South_African_Rugby_Union "South African Rugby Union") confirmed that the two South African teams that had been cut from the southern hemisphere [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") competition, [Cheetahs](/wiki/Cheetahs_(rugby_union) "Cheetahs (rugby union)") and [Southern Kings](/wiki/Southern_Kings "Southern Kings"), would be joining an expanded [Pro14](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") league ahead of the [2017–18 season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Pro14 "2017–18 Pro14"). Due to the addition of two new teams, the league format was changed to feature two conferences, A and B, with Munster being placed in conference A alongside [Cardiff Blues](/wiki/Cardiff_Rugby "Cardiff Rugby"), [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)"), [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors"), [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht_Rugby "Connacht Rugby"), [Cheetahs](/wiki/Cheetahs_(rugby_union) "Cheetahs (rugby union)") and [Zebre Parma](/wiki/Zebre_Parma "Zebre Parma").[[145]](#cite_note-146)

In October 2017, Munster confirmed that then-[South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team") forwards coach [Johann van Graan](/wiki/Johann_van_Graan "Johann van Graan") would join the province in November as their new head coach.[[146]](#cite_note-147) In the same month, it was announced that Munster's all-time leading try scorer, [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo"), would leave the province at the end of the season.[[147]](#cite_note-148) In rounds 1 and 2 of the [2017–18 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup_pool_stage#Pool_4 "2017–18 European Rugby Champions Cup pool stage") in October 2017, Munster drew 17–17 away to [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique") before earning a 14–7 win at home to [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92").[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150) Erasmus and Nienaber left Munster in early November 2017, with the province confirming their departure on 13 November 2017.[[150]](#cite_note-151) Johann van Graan's first official game as Munster's new head coach was a 36–19 win away against [Zebre Parma](/wiki/Zebre_Parma "Zebre Parma") in the Pro14 on 26 November 2017.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153) [Lions](/wiki/Lions_(United_Rugby_Championship) "Lions (United Rugby Championship)") defence coach [JP Ferreira](/wiki/JP_Ferreira "JP Ferreira") joined Munster in December 2017.[[153]](#cite_note-154) In the December Champions Cup double-header against [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers"), Munster won 33–10 at home before winning 25–16 away, their first win at [Welford Road](/wiki/Welford_Road_Stadium "Welford Road Stadium") for 11 years.[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156) In doing so, Munster became the first team to defeat Leicester in both games of the double-header since they were introduced in 1999, while the home victory also saw Munster surpass 4,000 points in the competition.[[156]](#cite_note-Stats_&_Pics:_Season_So_Far-157) In rounds 5 and 6 of the Champions Cup, Munster lost 34–30 away to [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92") before beating [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique") 48–3 at home, securing a record 17th quarter-final.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159) Munster beat 3-time tournament champions [Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") 20–19 in the quarter-final after a late [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway") try and conversion from [Ian Keatley](/wiki/Ian_Keatley "Ian Keatley").[[159]](#cite_note-160) In the semi-final, Munster were beaten 27–22 by their French pool 4 opponents Racing 92.[[160]](#cite_note-161)

Munster finished 2nd in conference A of the [2017–18 Pro14](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Pro14 "2017–18 Pro14") season on 69 points. In their semi-final qualifier against [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") on 5 May 2018, Munster won 20–16 to progress to a semi-final away from home.[[161]](#cite_note-162) In the semi-final against recently crowned [Champions Cup](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2017–18 European Rugby Champions Cup") winners and arch-rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") on 19 May 2018, Munster lost 16–15, bringing to an end their 2017–18 season.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

Munster played two pre-season fixtures ahead of the 2018–19 campaign, the first against [London Irish](/wiki/London_Irish "London Irish"), whose directory of rugby was former Munster and Ireland head coach [Declan Kidney](/wiki/Declan_Kidney "Declan Kidney"), and the second against [Exeter Chiefs](/wiki/Exeter_Chiefs "Exeter Chiefs"), which was the first meeting between the two clubs.[[163]](#cite_note-164) Munster beat London Irish 32–28, but lost 12–0 against Exeter Chiefs.[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[165]](#cite_note-166)

Drawn alongside French [Top 14](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Top_14_season "2017–18 Top 14 season") champions [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique") and English sides [Exeter Chiefs](/wiki/Exeter_Chiefs "Exeter Chiefs") and [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby"), Munster competed in pool 2 of the [2018–19 European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2018–19 European Rugby Champions Cup").[[166]](#cite_note-Tough_Draw_For_Munster-167) Munster opened their [2018–19 Pro14](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Pro14 "2018–19 Pro14") season with a six-try 38–0 victory against South African side [Cheetahs](/wiki/Cheetahs_(rugby_union) "Cheetahs (rugby union)") in [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") on 1 September 2018. [Rory Scannell](/wiki/Rory_Scannell "Rory Scannell"), [Dave Kilcoyne](/wiki/Dave_Kilcoyne "Dave Kilcoyne"), [Tommy O'Donnell](/wiki/Tommy_O%27Donnell "Tommy O'Donnell"), [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan"), [Dave O'Callaghan](/wiki/Dave_O%27Callaghan "Dave O'Callaghan") and [Man-of-the-Match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match") [Darren Sweetnam](/wiki/Darren_Sweetnam "Darren Sweetnam") scored the tries, with Hanrahan adding four conversions, in a match that saw [Arno Botha](/wiki/Arno_Botha "Arno Botha"), [Mike Haley](/wiki/Mike_Haley_(rugby_union) "Mike Haley (rugby union)"), [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery") and academy players [Shane Daly](/wiki/Shane_Daly "Shane Daly") and [Gavin Coombes](/wiki/Gavin_Coombes "Gavin Coombes") make their competitive debuts for the province, whilst [Neil Cronin](/wiki/Neil_Cronin "Neil Cronin") also made his first appearance for Munster since March 2015.[[167]](#cite_note-168)

[Tadhg Beirne](/wiki/Tadhg_Beirne "Tadhg Beirne") made his debut for Munster in the provinces 25–10 defeat away to [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors") on 7 September 2018.[[168]](#cite_note-169) Munster's 64–7 win against [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby") on 29 September 2018 was a record win for the province in the [Pro14](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship"); Munster's previous record win being 47–0 against [Zebre Parma](/wiki/Zebre_Parma "Zebre Parma") in 2016.[[169]](#cite_note-170) In round one of the [2018–19 Champions Cup](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup_pool_stage#Pool_2 "2018–19 European Rugby Champions Cup pool stage") on 13 October 2018, a try from Stander and five points from the boot of [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery") secured a 10–10 draw in [Sandy Park](/wiki/Sandy_Park "Sandy Park") against [Exeter Chiefs](/wiki/Exeter_Chiefs "Exeter Chiefs"), in a match in which [Dan Goggin](/wiki/Dan_Goggin_(rugby_union) "Dan Goggin (rugby union)") and [Neil Cronin](/wiki/Neil_Cronin "Neil Cronin") made their competitive European debuts, whilst [Tadhg Beirne](/wiki/Tadhg_Beirne "Tadhg Beirne"), who was [Man-of-the-Match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match"), [Mike Haley](/wiki/Mike_Haley_(rugby_union) "Mike Haley (rugby union)") and Joey Carbery made their European debuts for Munster.[[170]](#cite_note-171) One week later, tries from [Mike Haley](/wiki/Mike_Haley_(rugby_union) "Mike Haley (rugby union)"), [Rhys Marshall](/wiki/Rhys_Marshall "Rhys Marshall"), [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery"), who also kicked four conversions and one penalty in a Man-of-the-Match performance, [Sammy Arnold](/wiki/Sammy_Arnold "Sammy Arnold") and [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway") helped Munster to a 36–22 bonus-point victory at home against [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby").[[171]](#cite_note-172)

In the December 2018 back-to-back Champions Cup fixtures against [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique"), Munster won 30–5 in round 3 on 9 December, with tries from [Rory Scannell](/wiki/Rory_Scannell "Rory Scannell"), Stander and [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan"), who also scored 15 points off the kicking tee. In the return leg away to Castres on 15 December, the French side won 13–12, with Joey Carbery scoring all of Munster's points from penalties.[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174) In round 5 of the Champions Cup, Munster beat [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Rugby "Gloucester Rugby") 41–15 away from home on 11 January 2019, with the tries coming from [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery") (2), [Rory Scannell](/wiki/Rory_Scannell "Rory Scannell"), [Keith Earls](/wiki/Keith_Earls "Keith Earls") and [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway") and 16 points of the kicking tee from Carbery.[[174]](#cite_note-175) In round 6, Munster beat [Exeter Chiefs](/wiki/Exeter_Chiefs "Exeter Chiefs") 9–7 in [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") on 19 January 2019 to advance to a record 18th Champions Cup quarter-final. Joey Carbery scored all of Munster's points from penalties in a closely fought, physical game.[[175]](#cite_note-176) Munster defeated [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") 17–13 in their quarter-final in [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") on 30 March 2019 to advance to a 14th Champions Cup semi-final,[[176]](#cite_note-177) which Munster lost 32–16 to [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") in the [Ricoh Arena](/wiki/Ricoh_Arena "Ricoh Arena") on 20 April 2019.[[177]](#cite_note-178)

Munster finished second in conference A in the [2018–19 Pro14](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Pro14 "2018–19 Pro14") season, with 21 wins and 5 defeats,[[178]](#cite_note-179) and beat Italian side [Benetton](/wiki/Benetton_Rugby "Benetton Rugby") 15–13 in their quarter-final on 4 May 2019, to secure a semi-final against provincial rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") in the [RDS](/wiki/RDS_Arena "RDS Arena") on 18 May 2019.[[179]](#cite_note-180) which Leinster won 24–9, bringing to an end Munster's 2018–19 season.[[180]](#cite_note-181)

### 2019–2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2019–2022")]

Backs coach [Felix Jones](/wiki/Felix_Jones_(rugby_union) "Felix Jones (rugby union)") and forwards coach [Jerry Flannery](/wiki/Jerry_Flannery "Jerry Flannery") left the province when their contracts expired in June 2019.[[181]](#cite_note-182) [Graham Rowntree](/wiki/Graham_Rowntree "Graham Rowntree") joined the province as their new forwards coach after the completion of his duties with [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_rugby_union_team "Georgia national rugby union team") at the [2019 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2019_Rugby_World_Cup "2019 Rugby World Cup").[[182]](#cite_note-Rowntree_Munster-183) [Stephen Larkham](/wiki/Stephen_Larkham "Stephen Larkham"), attack coach for the [Australian national team](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team"), also joined the province as a senior coach ahead of the 2019–20 season.[[183]](#cite_note-Larkham_Munster-184)

Club legend and head of commercial and marketing, [Doug Howlett](/wiki/Doug_Howlett "Doug Howlett"), also left the province to return to [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") in the summer of 2019, ending his 11-year association with Munster.[[184]](#cite_note-185) Munster's [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") [Garrett Fitzgerald](/wiki/Garrett_Fitzgerald_(rugby_union) "Garrett Fitzgerald (rugby union)") retired upon reaching retirement age in June 2019. Fitzgerald was Munster's first CEO and had been in the post since 1999, making him the longest serving provincial chief executive in Irish rugby at the time.[[185]](#cite_note-186) Ian Flanagan, who was born in [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)") and previously worked at [Leicester City F.C](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."), was appointed to replace Fitzgerald.[[1]](#cite_note-Flanagan_Munster_CEO-2)

In a change from the previous two seasons, Munster were in conference B for the [2019–20 Pro14](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Pro14 "2019–20 Pro14") season, alongside [Benetton](/wiki/Benetton_Rugby "Benetton Rugby"), [Cardiff Blues](/wiki/Cardiff_Rugby "Cardiff Rugby"), [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht_Rugby "Connacht Rugby"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby"), [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") and [Southern Kings](/wiki/Southern_Kings "Southern Kings"). This will also be the case for the 2020–21 season, and the change was made based on the points total each club achieved after the completion of the regular [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Pro14 "2018–19 Pro14") season.[[186]](#cite_note-187)

Munster were seeded in tier 2 when the draw for the 2019–20 European Rugby Champions Cup was made in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") on Wednesday 19 June 2019,[[187]](#cite_note-188) and were drawn in pool 4 alongside defending champions [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C."), [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92"), who count former Munster players [Donnacha Ryan](/wiki/Donnacha_Ryan "Donnacha Ryan") and [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo") amongst their squad, and fellow [Pro14](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") side [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)").[[188]](#cite_note-189)

Munster opened their 2019–20 Pro14 season with a 39–9 home victory against Welsh side [Dragons](/wiki/Dragons_RFC "Dragons RFC") on 28 September 2019, with the tries coming from [Arno Botha](/wiki/Arno_Botha "Arno Botha"), [Jack O'Donoghue](/wiki/Jack_O%27Donoghue "Jack O'Donoghue"), [Man-of-the-Match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match") [Shane Daly](/wiki/Shane_Daly "Shane Daly"), [Tyler Bleyendaal](/wiki/Tyler_Bleyendaal "Tyler Bleyendaal") and academy member [Diarmuid Barron](/wiki/Diarmuid_Barron "Diarmuid Barron"), and fly-half [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") contributing 14 points off the kicking tee. New signing [Nick McCarthy](/wiki/Nick_McCarthy_(rugby_union) "Nick McCarthy (rugby union)") and academy members [Keynan Knox](/wiki/Keynan_Knox "Keynan Knox") and [Jack O'Sullivan](/wiki/Jack_O%27Sullivan_(rugby_union) "Jack O'Sullivan (rugby union)") all made their senior competitive debuts for the province, and hooker [Kevin O'Byrne](/wiki/Kevin_O%27Byrne "Kevin O'Byrne") won his 50th cap.[[189]](#cite_note-190) Short-term signing [Jed Holloway](/wiki/Jed_Holloway "Jed Holloway") made his debut for the province in their 31–20 away win against South African side [Southern Kings](/wiki/Southern_Kings "Southern Kings") on 5 October 2019.[[190]](#cite_note-191)

In the opening two rounds of the [2019–20 Champions Cup](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup_pool_stage#Pool_4 "2019–20 European Rugby Champions Cup pool stage"), Munster beat Welsh [Pro14](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") rivals [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") 32–13 away from home on 16 November 2019, with tries from [Jeremy Loughman](/wiki/Jeremy_Loughman "Jeremy Loughman"), [Keith Earls](/wiki/Keith_Earls "Keith Earls"), [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway") and [James Cronin](/wiki/James_Cronin_(rugby_union) "James Cronin (rugby union)") and twelve points off the kicking tee from [Tyler Bleyendaal](/wiki/Tyler_Bleyendaal "Tyler Bleyendaal"),[[191]](#cite_note-192) before drawing 21–21 at home against French side [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92") on 23 November 2019; Munster's tries came from [Keith Earls](/wiki/Keith_Earls "Keith Earls") and [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway"), and [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") contributed eleven points with the boot. The draw was Munster's first at home in the Champions Cup, against a Racing side that included former Munster players [Donnacha Ryan](/wiki/Donnacha_Ryan "Donnacha Ryan") and [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo") in their starting XV.[[192]](#cite_note-193)

Prop [Stephen Archer](/wiki/Stephen_Archer "Stephen Archer") won his 200th cap for Munster in their [2019–20 Pro14](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Pro14 "2019–20 Pro14") round 7 fixture against [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") on 29 November 2019, becoming the eleventh player to achieve the accolade for the province. Academy fly-half [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") made his debut for Munster during the same game, scoring 11 points in the 18–16 defeat to the Scottish side.[[193]](#cite_note-194)

In the Champions Cup back-to-backs against defending champions [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C."), Munster won 10–3 at home on 7 December 2019, with the English side picking up a losing bonus point.[[194]](#cite_note-195) In the return fixture one week later, Munster were beaten 15–6, with two tries in the final quarter securing the win for Saracens and denying Munster a losing bonus point.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

Following an incident that triggered a large brawl in Munster's second fixture against Saracens in December 2019 and a complaint to the [EPCR](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") from Saracens, in which Munster team doctor Jamie Kearns was accused of verbally abusing Saracens hooker [Jamie George](/wiki/Jamie_George "Jamie George"), an independent panel upheld the complaint and found that Kearns had breached the EPCR's disciplinary rules, handing Kearns a three-week ban, suspended for 12 months, and a fine of €2,000 to be paid immediately.[[196]](#cite_note-197)

Munster went into their round 5 Champions Cup clash away to [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92") on 12 January 2020 knowing that they had to win to keep alive their hopes of progressing to the quarter-finals of the tournament, but despite leading the French club with ten minutes to go, late tries from Racing secured a 39–22 win for the home side.[[197]](#cite_note-198) Munster needed results elsewhere to go their way if they were to stand any chance of qualifying for the quarter-finals as the final round of pool matches took place, but [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors") 45–7 win against [Sale Sharks](/wiki/Sale_Sharks "Sale Sharks") ended those hopes before Munster's final game against [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") had even taken place.[[198]](#cite_note-199) In the event, Munster won 33–6 against Ospreys, with [Craig Casey](/wiki/Craig_Casey "Craig Casey"), who made his European debut for the province in the defeat to Racing, scoring his first try for Munster, and [Calvin Nash](/wiki/Calvin_Nash "Calvin Nash"), [Jack O'Sullivan](/wiki/Jack_O%27Sullivan_(rugby_union) "Jack O'Sullivan (rugby union)") and [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") making their European debuts for the province.[[199]](#cite_note-200)

Munster's 68–3 win against South African side [Southern Kings](/wiki/Southern_Kings "Southern Kings") in round 11 of the Pro14 on 14 February 2020 was a record margin of victory for the province in the competition, and the ten tries scored also set a new record for the province. Academy member [John Hodnett](/wiki/John_Hodnett "John Hodnett") made his debut for Munster in the fixture, scoring a try and earning the [Man-of-the-Match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match") award.[[200]](#cite_note-201) The match had an added poignancy for Munster, as their long-serving former CEO, [Garrett Fitzgerald](/wiki/Garrett_Fitzgerald_(rugby_union) "Garrett Fitzgerald (rugby union)"), who had only retired in June 2019, died following a battle with illness earlier that day.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

The [2019–20 Pro14](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Pro14 "2019–20 Pro14") was suspended indefinitely by tournament organisers on 12 March 2020 in response to the ongoing [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[202]](#cite_note-203) Munster's round 14 and 15 fixtures against Italian side [Benetton](/wiki/Benetton_Rugby "Benetton Rugby") had already been postponed.[[203]](#cite_note-Munster_Benetton_Postponed-204) [Tyler Bleyendaal](/wiki/Tyler_Bleyendaal "Tyler Bleyendaal") was forced to retire from playing rugby with immediate effect in May 2020 due to a persistent neck injury.[[204]](#cite_note-Tyler_Bleyendaal_Retires-205) In the same month, prop [Brian Scott](/wiki/Brian_Scott_(rugby_union) "Brian Scott (rugby union)") was also forced to retire with immediate effect due to injury.[[205]](#cite_note-Brian_Scott_Retires-206) The regular season resumed on 22 August 2020, with the number of rounds reduced from 21 to 15 and any games postponed prior to the indefinite suspension of the season being deemed as 0–0 draws and both teams awarded two points. Rounds 14 and 15 took place as derbies in each territory, with the top two teams in each conference progressing to a semi-final stage.[[206]](#cite_note-Pro14_Restart_Date-207)

Munster resumed their season on 22 August 2020 with a fixture against [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") in the [Aviva Stadium](/wiki/Aviva_Stadium "Aviva Stadium"), which Leinster won 27–25. Munster handed debuts to new signings [Damian de Allende](/wiki/Damian_de_Allende "Damian de Allende") and [RG Snyman](/wiki/RG_Snyman "RG Snyman"), though Snyman's first appearance for the province lasted only 7 minutes after he was injured during a lineout. [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway") scored tries either side of [Keith Earls](/wiki/Keith_Earls "Keith Earls")' try, with [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") kicking 10 points off the tee, and [Chris Farrell](/wiki/Chris_Farrell "Chris Farrell") won the [Man-of-the-Match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match") award.[[207]](#cite_note-208)

Munster completed their reduced 15 round Pro14 regular season with a seven try 49–12 win against [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht_Rugby "Connacht Rugby"), a victory that secured a semi-final against defending champions and provincial rivals Leinster on 4 September 2020. The tries came from [Chris Cloete](/wiki/Chris_Cloete "Chris Cloete"), [Jeremy Loughman](/wiki/Jeremy_Loughman "Jeremy Loughman"), [Tadhg Beirne](/wiki/Tadhg_Beirne "Tadhg Beirne"), [James Cronin](/wiki/James_Cronin_(rugby_union) "James Cronin (rugby union)"), two from [Andrew Conway](/wiki/Andrew_Conway "Andrew Conway") and a [penalty try](/wiki/Try_(rugby)#Penalty_try "Try (rugby)"), with [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") kicking all five of his conversions and [Rory Scannell](/wiki/Rory_Scannell "Rory Scannell") converting the final try. As well as getting on the scoresheet, Tadhg Beirne also won the Man-of-the-Match award on his first game back after fracturing an ankle against Saracens in December 2019.[[208]](#cite_note-209) Leinster won the semi-final 13–3, knocking Munster out at the semi-final stage of the Pro14 for the third season in a row.[[209]](#cite_note-210)

Munster opened their [2020–21 Pro14](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Pro14 "2020–21 Pro14") season with a 30–27 away win against [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets") on 3 October 2020. Despite nine penalties from Scarlets fullback [Leigh Halfpenny](/wiki/Leigh_Halfpenny "Leigh Halfpenny") and a red card for captain [Peter O'Mahony](/wiki/Peter_O%27Mahony "Peter O'Mahony"), tries from [Jack O'Donoghue](/wiki/Jack_O%27Donoghue "Jack O'Donoghue") and [Chris Farrell](/wiki/Chris_Farrell "Chris Farrell") kept Munster within touching distance of the hosts, and a try from replacement hooker [Kevin O'Byrne](/wiki/Kevin_O%27Byrne "Kevin O'Byrne"), converted by [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)"), levelled the score going into the final minutes of the game, before academy fly-half Healy scored a 50-metre penalty in the 81st minute to earn what had previously looked like an unlikely win for the province.[[210]](#cite_note-211)

Munster's [2020–21 Champions Cup](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2020–21 European Rugby Champions Cup") campaign commenced with a 21–7 home win against Harlequins on 13 December 2020, in which [Gavin Coombes](/wiki/Gavin_Coombes "Gavin Coombes"), [Damian de Allende](/wiki/Damian_de_Allende "Damian de Allende") and [Josh Wycherley](/wiki/Josh_Wycherley "Josh Wycherley") made their tournament debuts. Coombes scored one try, with the other being a penalty try, with [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") and [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") adding nine points of the kicking tee between them.[[211]](#cite_note-212) Munster travelled away to Clermont for round two on 19 December 2020 and, despite trailing 28–9 to the home side at one point, fought back to earn a stunning 39–31 win at the [Stade Marcel-Michelin](/wiki/Stade_Marcel-Michelin "Stade Marcel-Michelin"). The tries for Munster came from [Mike Haley](/wiki/Mike_Haley_(rugby_union) "Mike Haley (rugby union)"), [star-of-the-match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match") [CJ Stander](/wiki/CJ_Stander "CJ Stander") and [Kevin O'Byrne](/wiki/Kevin_O%27Byrne "Kevin O'Byrne"), with JJ Hanrahan scoring a perfect nine from nine off the kicking tee for the other 24 points.[[212]](#cite_note-213)

In early January 2021, the [EPCR](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") took the decision to temporarily suspend rounds 3 and 4 of the 2020–21 Champions Cup, following a directive from authorities in France that French clubs should not participate in the scheduled matches in response to health risks posed by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[213]](#cite_note-214)

Munster's 20–17 win against [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht_Rugby "Connacht Rugby") in round 14 of the 2020–21 Pro14 on 5 March 2021 saw them become the first team to qualify for [2021 Pro14 Grand Final](/wiki/2021_Pro14_Grand_Final "2021 Pro14 Grand Final"), as the victory gave them an unassailable 12 point lead at the top of conference B with two rounds remaining.[[214]](#cite_note-215) Munster were beaten 16–6 by arch-rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") in the [2021 Pro14 Grand Final](/wiki/2021_Pro14_Grand_Final "2021 Pro14 Grand Final") on 27 March 2021.[[215]](#cite_note-216)

The Champions Cup resumed on the weekend of 2/3/4 April 2021 with the top eight teams from each pool at the time of suspension progressing to the round of 16, where Munster had home advantage thanks to their wins in the opening games against Harlequins and Clermont.[[216]](#cite_note-217) Munster were drawn against [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain").[[217]](#cite_note-218)

In an enthralling encounter at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), Munster led 16–9 at half-time thanks to two tries from [Keith Earls](/wiki/Keith_Earls "Keith Earls") and two penalties from [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery"), but Toulouse pulled level thanks to a converted try from [Matthis Lebel](/wiki/Matthis_Lebel "Matthis Lebel"). [Gavin Coombes](/wiki/Gavin_Coombes "Gavin Coombes") scored from close-range to give Munster the lead again, before Toulouse captain [Julien Marchand](/wiki/Julien_Marchand "Julien Marchand") responded with a try to level the scores again at 23–23. Substitute fly-half [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") scored a penalty to give Munster a 26–23 lead heading into the final 15 minutes of the match, but Toulouse's talismanic scrum-half [Antoine Dupont](/wiki/Antoine_Dupont "Antoine Dupont") scored two tries in 9 minutes to help the French club pull away on the scoreboard. A late consolation try from Gavin Coombes, his second of the match, in overtime meant the final score was 40–33 to Toulouse, who became just the second French club to win a European match at Thomond Park and advanced to an away quarter-final against Munster's pool opponents [Clermont](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne").[[218]](#cite_note-219)

Munster opened their [2021–22 United Rugby Championship](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_United_Rugby_Championship "2021–22 United Rugby Championship") campaign with a bonus-point 42–17 win against the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)"), one of the four new South African teams, on 25 September 2021. [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo"), making his return for the province, scored two tries, extending his club record to 62, with [Gavin Coombes](/wiki/Gavin_Coombes "Gavin Coombes") continuing his try-scoring exploits from the previous season with two tries of his own. [Chris Cloete](/wiki/Chris_Cloete "Chris Cloete") and [player of the match](/wiki/Player_of_the_match "Player of the match") [Craig Casey](/wiki/Craig_Casey "Craig Casey") also crossed the try line. Fly-half [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery") scored seven points off the kicking tee, with his replacement [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") contributing five points off the tee in the second-half. [RG Snyman](/wiki/RG_Snyman "RG Snyman") made his eagerly-awaited return from long-term injury as a second-half replacement, and scrum-half [Rowan Osborne](/wiki/Rowan_Osborne_(rugby_union) "Rowan Osborne (rugby union)") made his competitive debut for the province, in a match that saw fans return to [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") for the first time since February 2020.[[219]](#cite_note-220)

Munster were in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") for rounds 6 and 7 of the United Rugby Championship, in which they were due to play the [Bulls](/wiki/Bulls_(rugby_union) "Bulls (rugby union)") and the [Lions](/wiki/Lions_(United_Rugby_Championship) "Lions (United Rugby Championship)"), however, the emergence of the *[omicron](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant")* variant of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") led to travel restrictions being implemented by [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") authorities, meaning the fixtures had to be postponed.[[220]](#cite_note-SA_games_postponed-221) 34 players and staff arrived back in Ireland on 1 December 2021 and immediately entered 10 days of mandatory self-isolation. 14 players and staff who had tested positive for COVID-19 had to remain in South Africa.[[221]](#cite_note-222)

In the wake of this disruption for Munster and other clubs, and with Munster's opening Champions Cup fixture away to [Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") falling on 12 December, the [EPCR](/wiki/European_Professional_Club_Rugby "European Professional Club Rugby") extended the deadline for registering players for the tournament to 8 December, meaning Munster could look to supplement their squad with short-term signings,[[222]](#cite_note-223)[[223]](#cite_note-224) and 22 players were subsequently registered with the province's Champions Cup squad.[[224]](#cite_note-Munster_Additional_Players-225)

A depleted Munster squad made up of internationals who'd been away with Ireland during the Autumn tests, academy players and members of the national and provincial talent squads travelled over to England to face Wasps on their opening Champions Cup fixture on 12 December 2021. The team, which featured 12 debutantes, pulled off one of the great Munster away victories, defeating their opponents 35–14 in a thrilling contest in front of a large contingent of Munster fans who had made the journey to Coventry.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

Munster made it back-to-back wins in the Champions Cup with a gritty 19–13 win at home against French club Castres on 18 December 2021, with a 58th minute try from number 8 [Jack O'Donoghue](/wiki/Jack_O%27Donoghue "Jack O'Donoghue") and 14 points off the tee from fly-half [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") being enough for the province to see off their opposition.[[226]](#cite_note-227)

In the reverse fixture against [Castres](/wiki/Castres_Olympique "Castres Olympique") in round 3 of the Champions Cup on 14 January 2022, Munster earned a 16–13 away win thanks to a 77th minute try from [Gavin Coombes](/wiki/Gavin_Coombes "Gavin Coombes"), converted by rookie fly-half [Jack Crowley](/wiki/Jack_Crowley "Jack Crowley"), who was faultless off the kicking tee in his first European start for the province. The win secured a place in the knockout stage of the tournament for Munster.[[227]](#cite_note-228) Nine days later, Munster followed up their away win against Castres with a 45–7 home win against [Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC"), with the tries coming from [Jeremy Loughman](/wiki/Jeremy_Loughman "Jeremy Loughman"), [Conor Murray](/wiki/Conor_Murray "Conor Murray"), [Jack O'Donoghue](/wiki/Jack_O%27Donoghue "Jack O'Donoghue"), who was player of the match on the occasion of his 150th cap for Munster, [Rory Scannell](/wiki/Rory_Scannell "Rory Scannell") and [Simon Zebo](/wiki/Simon_Zebo "Simon Zebo"), who scored a brace to become Munster's all-time leading try-scorer in the Champions Cup and the leading Irish try-scorer in the competition overall. Fly-half [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") and his replacement on the day, [Jack Crowley](/wiki/Jack_Crowley "Jack Crowley"), were both 100% from the kicking tee, with Healy scoring four conversions and a penalty, and Crowley adding two late conversions. The win secured home advantage for Munster in the second leg of the round of 16.[[228]](#cite_note-229)

Munster faced English club [Exeter Chiefs](/wiki/Exeter_Chiefs "Exeter Chiefs") in the round of 16,[[229]](#cite_note-230) and lost the first leg of the tie 13–8 away to the Premiership club on 9 April 2022. Exeter took a 10–0 lead in to half-time after tries from [Stuart Hogg](/wiki/Stuart_Hogg "Stuart Hogg") and [Jacques Vermeulen](/wiki/Jacques_Vermeulen "Jacques Vermeulen"), before Munster fly-half [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") hit back with a penalty to reduce the home sides lead. Hogg responded for Exeter with a drop-goal, but a 66th minute try from [Shane Daly](/wiki/Shane_Daly "Shane Daly") brought Munster back within touching distance on the scoreboard, and Exeter had to withstand some intense offensive play from Munster going into the final ten minutes, with replacement scrum-half [Craig Casey](/wiki/Craig_Casey "Craig Casey") almost drawing the scores level, before the hosts themselves assaulted the Munster try-line in the last minutes of the match in an ultimately unsuccessful attempt to extend their lead.[[230]](#cite_note-231)

Needing to overturn a five-point deficit to advance in the competition, Munster welcomed Exeter to Thomond Park for the second leg of their last 16 tie one week later. Fly-half [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery"), returning from injury, opened the scoring for the hosts with a penalty, but the visitors responded with their first try of the game to take an early 5–3 lead, before Carbery struck back for Munster with a try of his own, to which he added a second penalty to give Munster a 13–5 half-time lead. Exeter struck first in the second-half with a try in the 47th minute, but Carbery kept Munster in front with two further penalties, before centre [Damian de Allende](/wiki/Damian_de_Allende "Damian de Allende") scored a 74th minute try to secure a 26–10 home win for Munster and a 34–23 victory on aggregate, ensuring Munster progressed to a record 19th Champions Cup quarter-final.[[231]](#cite_note-232)

Facing defending champions [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain"), who knocked the province out of the previous season's tournament, in the quarter-final,[[232]](#cite_note-233) Munster began strongly with an 11th minute [Alex Kendellen](/wiki/Alex_Kendellen "Alex Kendellen") try, converted by [Joey Carbery](/wiki/Joey_Carbery "Joey Carbery") to lead 7–0, but [Romain Ntamack](/wiki/Romain_Ntamack "Romain Ntamack") hit back for Toulouse two minutes later with a try of his own, converted by [Thomas Ramos](/wiki/Thomas_Ramos_(rugby_union) "Thomas Ramos (rugby union)"), to level the scores. The visitors were dominating the scrum and scored their second try in the 25th minute when [Matthis Lebel](/wiki/Matthis_Lebel "Matthis Lebel") touched down, with Ramos again converting to give Toulouse a 14–7 lead. However, Munster struck back with a [Keith Earls](/wiki/Keith_Earls "Keith Earls") try just before half-time which Carbery converted to leave the scores level heading into the break. The home side came out firing in the second-half, with [Mike Haley](/wiki/Mike_Haley_(rugby_union) "Mike Haley (rugby union)") scoring a 43rd minute try, again converted by Carbery, to give Munster a 21–14 lead, which Carbery extended with a penalty in the 56th minute, but Toulouse responded with Lebel's second try in the 66th minute which, when converted by Ramos, reduced the margin to just three points heading into the final ten minutes.
