A 75th-minute penalty from Ramos levelled the scores at 24–24, and that remained the score at full-time after [Ben Healy](/wiki/Ben_Healy_(rugby_union) "Ben Healy (rugby union)") missed with a 56-metre penalty attempt in the final minute of normal time.[[233]](#cite_note-Munster_Toulouse_Penalties-234)

With the scores level at full-time and as both teams scored three tries, [extra time](/wiki/Overtime_(sports)#Rugby_union "Overtime (sports)") ensued, played over two ten-minute halves, but even after that the two teams could not be separated after three missed drop goals between the two, and the game went to a penalty shootout. Munster went first and [Conor Murray](/wiki/Conor_Murray "Conor Murray") scored his penalty. [Antoine Dupont](/wiki/Antoine_Dupont "Antoine Dupont") responded successfully for Toulouse, but Ben Healy missed his first attempt, and when Thomas Ramos scored his penalty, it was advantage Toulouse. Carbery scored his penalty, but Romain Ntamack scored his own to restore Toulouse's lead. Murray missed with his second attempt, whilst Dupont was successful with his own to give Toulouse a 4–2 lead, meaning Healy had to score with his second attempt to give Munster any hope, but he was unable to do so, and Toulouse advanced to the semi-finals, knocking Munster out of the competition for the second season in a row.[[233]](#cite_note-Munster_Toulouse_Penalties-234)

A 35–25 defeat away to [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") in round 18 of the [2021–22 United Rugby Championship](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_United_Rugby_Championship "2021–22 United Rugby Championship") meant that Munster finished sixth in the league overall, and third in the Irish Shield,[[234]](#cite_note-235) and the province headed north to face [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby") in the quarter-finals on 3 June 2022, but Munster lost 36–17 to bring the curtain down on their 2021–22 season, as well as [Johann van Graan](/wiki/Johann_van_Graan "Johann van Graan")'s tenure as head coach.[[235]](#cite_note-236)

### Graham Rowntree becomes head coach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Graham Rowntree becomes head coach")]

Head coach [Johann van Graan](/wiki/Johann_van_Graan "Johann van Graan"), who joined Munster in November 2017, confirmed in December 2021 that he would be leaving the province at the end of the 2021–22 season to join English club [Bath](/wiki/Bath_Rugby "Bath Rugby"). In addition to this, senior coach [Stephen Larkham](/wiki/Stephen_Larkham "Stephen Larkham") also left the province to return home to Australia to become head coach of the [Brumbies](/wiki/Brumbies "Brumbies"), and defence coach [JP Ferreira](/wiki/JP_Ferreira "JP Ferreira") followed van Graan to join Bath. However, forwards coach [Graham Rowntree](/wiki/Graham_Rowntree "Graham Rowntree") extended his stay with the province by a further two years,[[236]](#cite_note-Munster_Coaching_Changes-237) and the province confirmed in April 2022 that Rowntree would be promoted to head coach from the 2022–23 season.[[237]](#cite_note-Rowntree_promoted_to_head_coach-238) [Mike Prendergast](/wiki/Mike_Prendergast_(rugby_union) "Mike Prendergast (rugby union)"), a former scrum-half for the province, joined Rowntree's coaching setup as the attack coach on a three-year contract, having most recently fulfilled a similar role for French club [Racing 92](/wiki/Racing_92 "Racing 92").[[238]](#cite_note-Prendergast_Joins_Munster-239) [Andi Kyriacou](/wiki/Andi_Kyriacou "Andi Kyriacou"), who had joined the province in April 2021 as an elite player development officer with the academy, was promoted to forwards coach with the senior squad on a two-year contract,[[239]](#cite_note-240) and former Munster player [Denis Leamy](/wiki/Denis_Leamy "Denis Leamy") returned to the province as defence coach on a three-year contract.[[240]](#cite_note-241)

After a poor start to the season, Munster won the [2022–23 United Rugby Championship](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_United_Rugby_Championship "2022–23 United Rugby Championship") after beating defending champions the [Stormers](/wiki/Stormers "Stormers") 19–14 away from home in the [final](/wiki/2023_United_Rugby_Championship_final "2023 United Rugby Championship final") on 27 May 2023. In doing so, Munster ended a trophy drought that had lasted since their previous league title during the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Celtic_League "2010–11 Celtic League"), and secured the first silverware of Graham Rowntree's reign as head coach.[[241]](#cite_note-242)

## Colours and crest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Colours and crest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Flag_of_Munster.svg/250px-Flag_of_Munster.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Munster.svg)

Flag of the province of Munster

The 'three crowns' flag of Munster Rugby derives from the historic [coat of arms of Munster](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Munster "Coat of arms of Munster"), which reputedly alludes to the three constituent historic kingdoms of Munster; [Thomond](/wiki/Thomond "Thomond") in the north, [Desmond](/wiki/County_Desmond "County Desmond") in the south, and [Ormond](/wiki/Kings_of_Osraige "Kings of Osraige") in the east.

A revamped logo was introduced for the 2003–04 season which included the addition of a stag with the three crowns.[[247]](#cite_note-newcrest-261) The new crest was designed by the Limerick graphic design and branding agency *Designer's Ink*, who received a Gold Award in the Irish Design Effectiveness Awards for the branding and implementation of Munster Rugby.[[248]](#cite_note-crestdesign-262) The crest was designed to maintain the three crowns and the new red stag symbolises strength and competitiveness.[[247]](#cite_note-newcrest-261) The decision for change was a product of two years of planning of research and design.[[247]](#cite_note-newcrest-261)

Munster's kit is made by [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"), who replaced [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_of_New_Zealand "Canterbury of New Zealand") in 2007 in a deal initially covering kit supply for three seasons. Between 2004 and 2013, [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") was Munster's primary sponsor, appearing on the front of their jersey. On 23 May 2013, it was announced on that [Bank of Ireland](/wiki/Bank_of_Ireland "Bank of Ireland") would be replacing [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") as Munster's sponsor.[[249]](#cite_note-263) In April 2017, the deal with Bank of Ireland was extended until the end of the 2022–23 season.[[250]](#cite_note-264) In August 2017, Munster announced that [Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport") had become its 'official airport partner' in a three-year deal, which would see the Shannon Airport logo appear on the players' shorts from the 2017–18 season onwards.[[251]](#cite_note-265) The deals with kit manufacturer Adidas and retail partner LifeStyle Sports were extended in July 2019 until the end of the 2025–26 season.[[252]](#cite_note-266)

## Home grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Home grounds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Thomond_Park.jpg/250px-Thomond_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomond_Park.jpg)

[Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park")

Munster have two main stadia where they play their home matches – [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") in [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick") and [Musgrave Park](/wiki/Musgrave_Park,_Cork "Musgrave Park, Cork") in [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"). Thomond Park is the bigger of the two, with a capacity of 25,600, which can be expanded up to 26,267 with temporary seating, while Musgrave Park holds 8,800.[[253]](#cite_note-267) As well as Munster, [Shannon](/wiki/Shannon_RFC "Shannon RFC") and [UL Bohemians](/wiki/UL_Bohemians_R.F.C. "UL Bohemians R.F.C.") play at the grounds of Thomond Park. Thomond Park is famous for its atmosphere and unique history[[254]](#cite_note-thomond-268) – its noise during play and complete silence when a player (home or away) is kicking at goal. It was also famous for Munster's intimidating record that it held for over a decade – having never been beaten at home during the Heineken Cup. However, the record was broken during the [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Heineken_Cup "2006–07 Heineken Cup") season when they were defeated by Leicester Tigers.[[255]](#cite_note-Leicesterwin-269) Munster train in the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick").

Thomond Park went through a major renovation in 1999 and in 2006, Munster announced plans to upgrade it. In autumn 2008, the new 25,600 capacity stadium was opened. Two sweeping arches are one of the defining features of the stadium, as well as the concourse outside of the new East Stand. The new stadium design was well received and won the Public Choice Award for 2009 from the Irish Architecture Foundation. A long discussion and consultation on the new name concluded with the decision that the name would remain *Thomond Park*.[[256]](#cite_note-270)

## Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Supporters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Munster_rugby_2006.jpg/250px-Munster_rugby_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Munster_rugby_2006.jpg)

Thousands of fans watch the [2006 Heineken Cup Final](/wiki/2006_Heineken_Cup_Final "2006 Heineken Cup Final") in [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick").

The Munster Rugby Supporters Club was founded in 1999 and has branches in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), [London](/wiki/London "London") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[257]](#cite_note-271) The strength of Munster's support was demonstrated during Munster's [2006](/wiki/2006_Heineken_Cup_Final "2006 Heineken Cup Final") and [2008 Heineken Cup final](/wiki/2008_Heineken_Cup_Final "2008 Heineken Cup Final") wins. News reports detailed the lengths that fans were willing to go to secure tickets to the game, with some Munster fans travelling to Biarritz to buy up the French allocation of tickets. On the day of the game the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") was filled with a capacity crowd of 74,500. Of those numbers it is estimated that somewhere between 55,000 and 65,000 were Munster fans with the remainder being neutrals and [Biarritz](/wiki/Biarritz_Olympique "Biarritz Olympique") supporters. The Millennium Stadium was intended to be a neutral venue but commentators on the day remarked that it could hardly be counted as such.

Munster played in the most attended semi-final match of the Heineken Cup. 82,208 spectators attended their [2008–09 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Heineken_Cup "2008–09 Heineken Cup") semi-final against Irish rivals [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby"), which was played in [Croke Park](/wiki/Croke_Park "Croke Park"), Dublin. This was also, at the time, the largest crowd ever at a club rugby union match. The record was broken in a league game between English sides [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens_F.C. "Saracens F.C.") and [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") in 2012.

Munster's appearance in the [2002 final](/wiki/2002_Heineken_Cup_Final "2002 Heineken Cup Final") of the Heineken Cup against [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium"), which drew 74,600, was the record attendance for a final in the competition[[258]](#cite_note-272) until the [2007 Heineken Cup final](/wiki/2007_Heineken_Cup_Final "2007 Heineken Cup Final") between Leicester and [London Wasps](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC") at the newly expanded [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium"). Munster's 2005 quarter-final against [Biarritz Olympique](/wiki/Biarritz_Olympique "Biarritz Olympique") in [Estadio Anoeta](/wiki/Estadio_Anoeta "Estadio Anoeta"), played across the border in Spain in [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián") set the record for the biggest rugby match ever played in Spain with an attendance of 32,000.[[259]](#cite_note-sanseb-273) Their October 2006 Celtic League game against Leinster at Lansdowne Road beat the record for that competition with an attendance of 27,252.[[260]](#cite_note-274) This record lasted just two months however, with the [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") and [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_Rugby "Ulster Rugby") match on 31 December 2006 filling Lansdowne Road (over 48,000 in attendance) for the last match at the stadium before redevelopment.[[261]](#cite_note-275)

On 2 October 2010, Munster played [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster_Rugby "Leinster Rugby") in the round 5 of the [Celtic League](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship") at the [Aviva Stadium](/wiki/Aviva_Stadium "Aviva Stadium"), this set a new crowd attendance record for a Celtic League game at 50,645.[[262]](#cite_note-276) On 26 December 2017, a new attendance record for Thomond Park in the Pro14 was set when 26,267 were at the Munster v Leinster fixture.[[156]](#cite_note-Stats_&_Pics:_Season_So_Far-157)

Munster fans are known for their silence when a kick is being taken, but also for their noise. Fans repeatedly chant "MUNSTER" or sing "[The Fields of Athenry](/wiki/The_Fields_of_Athenry "The Fields of Athenry")" (an [Irish famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") song from Galway, Connacht) and "Stand Up and Fight" (from the Broadway musical *[Carmen Jones](/wiki/Carmen_Jones#Songs "Carmen Jones")*).[[263]](#cite_note-277) They sang "[The Black Velvet Band](/wiki/The_Black_Velvet_Band "The Black Velvet Band")" to the [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)")' Irish winger [Tommy Bowe](/wiki/Tommy_Bowe "Tommy Bowe") during their 2009 Heineken Cup quarter-final encounter. Tommy Bowe sang this song at the official reception for the [2009 Grand Slam](/wiki/2009_Six_Nations_Championship "2009 Six Nations Championship") winning Ireland rugby team. Munster Rugby has given the word "Garryowen" to the rugby lexicon. The Limerick club of [Garryowen](/wiki/Garryowen_Football_Club "Garryowen Football Club") introduced the "Garryowen kick", a high up-and-under which puts defending players under pressure. Shortly after the 2018 death of [Dolores O'Riordan](/wiki/Dolores_O%27Riordan "Dolores O'Riordan"), lead singer of [The Cranberries](/wiki/The_Cranberries "The Cranberries") (a band formed in Limerick), Munster fans adopted the band's song "[Zombie](/wiki/Zombie_(The_Cranberries_song) "Zombie (The Cranberries song)")" as an unofficial anthem.[[264]](#cite_note-278) The song's status was further cemented during the 2021–22 season, with the hit single featuring prominently in Munster's defeat against [Toulouse](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") in May 2022 and in their win against a [South Africa XV](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_union_team "South Africa national rugby union team") in [Páirc Uí Chaoimh](/wiki/P%C3%A1irc_U%C3%AD_Chaoimh "Páirc Uí Chaoimh") in November 2022.[[265]](#cite_note-279)[[266]](#cite_note-280)[[267]](#cite_note-281)

## Munster A

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Munster A")]

Munster A is the team that represents Munster in the [Celtic Cup](/wiki/Celtic_Cup_(2018_rugby_union_tournament) "Celtic Cup (2018 rugby union tournament)") and in the [IRFU Interprovincial Championship](/wiki/IRFU_Interprovincial_Championship "IRFU Interprovincial Championship"). Pre-professionalism and a formal league structure, the main Munster team competed in the IRFU Interprovincial Championship. Since the advent of professionalism, the provinces have fielded lesser teams in order to concentrate on the [league](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship"). The team is composed of senior Munster squad players requiring gametime, academy players and [All-Ireland League](/wiki/All-Ireland_League_(rugby_union) "All-Ireland League (rugby union)") players called up from their club.[[268]](#cite_note-282)

Having been beaten finalists in the [2009–10 British and Irish Cup](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_British_and_Irish_Cup "2009–10 British and Irish Cup"), losing 23–14 to [Cornish Pirates](/wiki/Cornish_Pirates "Cornish Pirates"),[[269]](#cite_note-283) Munster A secured success in the [British and Irish Cup](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Cup "British and Irish Cup") on 27 April 2012, beating [Cross Keys](/wiki/Cross_Keys_RFC "Cross Keys RFC") 31–12 in the final of the [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_British_and_Irish_Cup "2011–12 British and Irish Cup") tournament at Musgrave Park.[[270]](#cite_note-284) On 21 April 2017, Munster A won their second British and Irish Cup, beating English [RFU Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship") side [Jersey Reds](/wiki/Jersey_Reds "Jersey Reds") 29–28 in the [2016–17](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_British_and_Irish_Cup "2016–17 British and Irish Cup") final, which was held in Musgrave Park. At one point during the first half, Munster A had been losing 18–0, but fought back to secure victory.[[271]](#cite_note-285)

With 2017–18 being the last season in which the [British and Irish Cup](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Cup "British and Irish Cup") was held, the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") and [Irish Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union") formed a new tournament, the [Celtic Cup](/wiki/Celtic_Cup_(2018_rugby_union_tournament) "Celtic Cup (2018 rugby union tournament)"), which features development squads from the four Irish provinces and four Welsh regions, split into two pools of four. The first tournament ran over seven consecutive weeks between 7 September 2018 and 21 October 2018.[[272]](#cite_note-286)

### Golden Boot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Golden Boot")]

The United Rugby Championship Golden Boot is awarded to the kicker who has successfully converted the highest percentage of place kicks during the 18-week regular URC season. The prize has been awarded annually since 2012.

| Competition | Player | Success rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Pro12 "2013–14 Pro12")[[324]](#cite_note-342) | [JJ Hanrahan](/wiki/JJ_Hanrahan "JJ Hanrahan") | 88.71% |
| [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Pro14 "2019–20 Pro14")[[325]](#cite_note-343) | JJ Hanrahan | 90.91% |

### Playmaker of the Year

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Playmaker of the Year")]

The Playmaker award was first introduced in 2025 and is awarded to the player with the most points based upon their total number of try assists (3 points), offloads (2 points) and defenders beaten (1 point).[[326]](#cite_note-Playmaker-344)

| Competition | Player | Points |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [2024–25](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_United_Rugby_Championship "2024–25 United Rugby Championship")[[326]](#cite_note-Playmaker-344)[[327]](#cite_note-:2-345) | [Tom Farrell](/wiki/Tom_Farrell_(rugby_union) "Tom Farrell (rugby union)") | 158 points comes from five try assists (15 points), 41 offloads (82 points), and 61 defenders beaten (61 points) |

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munster_Rugby&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Thomond Park's official capacity is 25,600 but can be expanded up to 26,276 with temporary seating.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-252)** Formerly known as Heineken Cup
3. **[^](#cite_ref-253)** Formerly known as Celtic League/Magners League/Pro12/Pro14
4. **[^](#cite_ref-254)** Contested from 2009 to 2018
5. **[^](#cite_ref-255)** Contested from 2003 to 2005
6. **[^](#cite_ref-256)** Contested from 1946 to 2002 – Bold indicates Grand Slam; \* indicates shared title; years shown are season ending years
7. **[^](#cite_ref-258)** Contested from 2007 to 2008
8. **[^](#cite_ref-315)** Kleyn joined Munster in 2016 and qualified for Ireland based on residency laws, going on to earn five international caps for Ireland in 2019, before reverting his international affiliation back to South Africa in June 2023 following changes to World Rugby's eligibility laws in 2021.
